verdict_reasoning: C3 is a secreted complement protein, but upon activation it is cleaved to C3b which becomes covalently tethered to cell surfaces via its reactive thioester, forming a wash-resistant covalent bond with hydroxyl/amine groups on target cell membranes. This thioester-mediated covalent deposition onto cell surfaces is the canonical mechanism of opsonization and is the basis for therapeutics like pegcetacoplan. C3b/iC3b/C3d fragments are stably anchored to cell surfaces and accessible to binders (e.g., complement regulators, anti-C3b antibodies). This fits stable_surface_attachment via covalent thioester chemistry to a cell-surface acceptor.
